presets.label: YouTube Shorts
presets.notes: 9:16 or 1:1, classified as Shorts when ≤ 3 min.

sources.title: Understand three-minute YouTube Shorts
sources.excerpt: Vertical or square videos up to three minutes are categorized as Shorts (uploaded on or after Oct 15, 2024).

sources.title: YouTube recommended upload encoding settings
sources.excerpt: Common frame rates include: 24, 25, 30, 48, 50, 60 fps. Encode and upload in the same frame rate used when recording.

presets.label: YouTube (16:9)
presets.notes: Landscape master — crop in post from Open Gate. Verified accounts: up to 12 hr.

sources.title: YouTube recommended upload encoding settings
sources.excerpt: Common frame rates include: 24, 25, 30, 48, 50, 60 fps. Standard aspect ratio on desktop is 16:9.

sources.title: Upload videos longer than 15 minutes
sources.excerpt: Verified accounts can upload videos up to 12 hours long.

presets.label: Instagram Reels
presets.notes: Upload up to 20 min (rollout varies). In-app record often capped lower. Min 30 fps.

sources.title: Reel size and aspect ratios on Instagram
sources.excerpt: Aspect ratio 1.91:1–9:16. Minimum frame rate 30 FPS and minimum resolution 720 px.

sources.title: Instagram Graph API — IG User Media (Reels)
sources.excerpt: Reels support 9:16; consumer uploads up to 20 minutes (2025–2026 rollout, account-dependent).

presets.label: Instagram Stories
presets.notes: 60 seconds per Story segment; longer uploads are trimmed or split.

sources.title: Tips for uploading, recording and editing your Instagram story
sources.excerpt: Videos longer than 60 seconds can be edited with the in-app trimmer (split into segments).

sources.title: Reel size and aspect ratios on Instagram
sources.excerpt: Minimum frame rate 30 FPS applies to vertical video on Instagram; 9:16 is the Stories format.

presets.label: Instagram Feed
presets.notes: 4:5 feed portrait. Up to 60 min desktop; mobile uploads often cap at 15 min.

sources.title: Reel size and aspect ratios on Instagram
sources.excerpt: Minimum frame rate 30 FPS for vertical video on Instagram.

sources.title: Instagram media requirements (Meta for Developers)
sources.excerpt: Feed supports 4:5 aspect ratio; videos between 3 sec and 60 min; minimum width 600 px (1080×1350 is optimal 4:5).

presets.label: TikTok
presets.notes: In-app record up to 10 min; TikTok Studio web upload up to 60 min. No official FPS list.

sources.title: TikTok Creator Academy — TikTok Studio Web
sources.excerpt: Upload videos up to 60 minutes long in 1080p, 2K, or 4K via TikTok Studio Web (up to 30 GB).

sources.title: TikTok Support — Camera tools
sources.excerpt: In-app recording supports 15 s or 60 s segments; longer content is uploaded from device.

presets.label: Facebook Reels
presets.notes: 9:16 recommended. Since June 2025 Meta: no length cap (all videos → Reels).

sources.title: Requirements for Facebook Reels
sources.excerpt: Recommended resolution 1080p, frame rate 24–60 FPS. Since June 2025 all Facebook videos post as Reels with no length cap.

presets.label: LinkedIn
presets.notes: Landscape 1080p preferred. Up to 15 min desktop, 10 min mobile app.

sources.title: Video sharing troubleshooting
sources.excerpt: Frame rates 10–60 fps; max duration 15 min desktop / 10 min mobile. 25 fps is within the supported range.

sources.title: LinkedIn Live Video encoder settings
sources.excerpt: Recommended 30 fps and 16:9; 720p recommended, 1080p maximum for live.

presets.label: X (Twitter)
presets.notes: Free accounts: 2 min 20 sec. Premium: up to 4 hr on web/iOS (720p–1080p). Max 40 fps upload.

sources.title: How to share and watch videos on X
sources.excerpt: Free uploads up to 140 s / 512 MB. Max resolution 1920×1200; max frame rate 40 fps; max bitrate 25 Mbps.

sources.title: About longer videos for X Premium subscribers
sources.excerpt: Premium: up to 4 hours (1080p) on web/iOS; up to 10 min on Android.
